response_raw:
{
  "mode": "human",
  "topic": "How to manage and dealing with discussions about healthcare and COVID vaccines.",
  "opinion": "As a person who's lived through this whole pandemic thing, I've got my own way of handling the vaccine talk. It’s like trying to patch up pipes in your house—sometimes you’ve got leaks everywhere, but you gotta focus on one at a time.

I’m not much for sitting around talking about healthcare stuff because it gets so complicated with all those fancy terms and numbers that don’t mean nothing to me really. I work as a plumber fixing pipes, not dealing with big hospitals or clinics every day. My background is practical—things you can see and touch, like how tight the fittings are.

But when people start chatting about COVID vaccines on social media, which I do just occasionally because it keeps my mind off things sometimes (especially during depressive episodes), those conversations get messy fast. You’ve got folks saying all kinds of stuff based on misinformation or hearsay from their relatives who didn’t finish high school either.

The first thing that pops into my head is: how does this affect me personally? Like, if I don’t get vaccinated do they think plumbing jobs will suddenly disappear because everyone else gets sick around me? That’s the kind of thinking that keeps you grounded in reality. You look at your own life—your family responsibilities, maybe some anxiety about keeping the roof over your head—and connect it to what you hear about vaccines.

Then there’s my faith—I’m Christian so I put stock in science but also believe things come from God's will sometimes. That means listening carefully without getting too caught up on either side.

The key is being honest and straightforward—no pretending to understand all the jargon or statistics when you don’t—and not letting those discussions overwhelm you if they’re making you feel worse.",
  "reasoning_summary": "Start by connecting personal well-being with health topics, then consider faith-based perspectives without overcomplication, use simple analogies for complex ideas, maintain a grounded approach in daily life and responsibilities rather than abstract debates, avoid getting bogged down in technical details which may trigger depressive episodes or anxiety.",
  "tone": "Pragmatic, straightforward",
  "citations": ["Personal Experience"],
  "safety_notes": "None",
  "used_persona_fields":["age",54,"occupation","plumber","employment_status","employed part-time","education_level","upper secondary","psychological_feature","depressed","religious_beliefs","Christianity","time_onsocialmedia","You spend a few times monthly on social media"]
}

response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with discussions about healthcare and COVID vaccines.
response_parsed.opinion:
As a person who's lived through this whole pandemic thing, I've got my own way of handling the vaccine talk. It’s like trying to patch up pipes in your house—sometimes you’ve got leaks everywhere, but you gotta focus on one at a time.

I’m not much for sitting around talking about healthcare stuff because it gets so complicated with all those fancy terms and numbers that don’t mean nothing to me really. I work as a plumber fixing pipes, not dealing with big hospitals or clinics every day. My background is practical—things you can see and touch, like how tight the fittings are.

But when people start chatting about COVID vaccines on social media, which I do just occasionally because it keeps my mind off things sometimes (especially during depressive episodes), those conversations get messy fast. You’ve got folks saying all kinds of stuff based on misinformation or hearsay from their relatives who didn’t finish high school either.

The first thing that pops into my head is: how does this affect me personally? Like, if I don’t get vaccinated do they think plumbing jobs will suddenly disappear because everyone else gets sick around me? That’s the kind of thinking that keeps you grounded in reality. You look at your own life—your family responsibilities, maybe some anxiety about keeping the roof over your head—and connect it to what you hear about vaccines.

Then there’s my faith—I’m Christian so I put stock in science but also believe things come from God's will sometimes. That means listening carefully without getting too caught up on either side.

The key is being honest and straightforward—no pretending to understand all the jargon or statistics when you don’t—and not letting those discussions overwhelm you if they’re making you feel worse.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: Start by connecting personal well-being with health topics, then consider faith-based perspectives without overcomplication, use simple analogies for complex ideas, maintain a grounded approach in daily life and responsibilities rather than abstract debates, avoid getting bogged down in technical details which may trigger depressive episodes or anxiety.
used_persona_fields: You spend a few times monthly on social media
